text.title: Retention Loop Patterns
text.summary: Six self-reinforcing loop patterns that drive users to return without relying solely on external notifications.

rule.statement: Diagnose which loop type is operative in your product before redesigning anything; applying a content loop pattern to a workflow tool, or gamification to a B2B multi-stakeholder product, produces no retention lift and erodes product trust.
rule.rationale: Each loop type requires specific product mechanics. Mismatched patterns consume engineering resources without addressing the actual return-driver for the user.

section.title: Content Loop
section.p: New content creates return visits. Flow: user visits → consumes content → algorithm learns → better content next time → user returns. Examples: TikTok, Netflix, YouTube. Key mechanics: infinite scroll, personalization, creator ecosystem, new-content notifications.

section.title: Social Loop
section.p: Friends' activity triggers returns. Flow: user posts → friends see → friends react → user notified → user returns to engage → friends notified. Examples: Facebook, Instagram, Slack. Key mechanics: activity feed, @mentions, read receipts, typing indicators.

section.title: Progress Loop
section.p: Accumulated progress creates loss aversion that drives return. Flow: user takes action → progress recorded → streak or level increases → user invested → returns to maintain or grow. Examples: Duolingo, Fitbit, GitHub. Key mechanics: streaks, XP/levels, progress visualization, loss aversion messaging.

section.title: Stored Value Loop
section.p: User data makes the product more valuable over time, creating pull to return. Flow: user adds data → product becomes more valuable → user depends on it → returns to access or add more. Examples: Notion, Dropbox, CRM tools. Key mechanics: easy capture, search/organization, reminders about stored items.

section.title: Workflow Loop
section.p: Work assignments create mandatory return. Flow: work assigned → team expects completion → user must return → new work created. Examples: Jira, Asana, Linear. Key mechanics: task assignments, due dates, team notifications, blocked-work visibility.

section.title: Network Effect Loop
section.p: Value increases with each additional user, raising switching cost. Flow: user joins → invites colleagues → more users = more value → everyone depends on product → high switching cost = retention. Examples: Slack, Zoom, Figma. Key mechanics: collaboration features, easy invites, shared workspaces.
